# Clinical Applications of LLMs

## Clinical Decision Support

The deployment of LLMs as clinical decision support tools has generated a rapidly expanding body of evidence, with performance comparisons against human clinicians revealing both striking capabilities and important limitations. A landmark prospective cross-sectional study among 1,106 emergency physicians in Turkey evaluated ChatGPT 4.0 and Gemini 2.5 on ten NSTEMI-related multiple-choice questions derived from the 2023 European Society of Cardiology guidelines; both AI models correctly answered nine of ten questions, significantly outperforming the physician cohort (mean 7.62 ± 1.32 correct answers) [@Yorgancolu2025Performance]. These results, while striking, were obtained under controlled conditions without real clinical context, underscoring the need for caution when extrapolating to bedside practice.

A contrasting picture emerges when LLMs are challenged with subspecialty knowledge. Claude and ChatGPT-4 achieved only 32% and 23% accuracy, respectively, on 100 custom cardiothoracic anesthesia board-style questions—both far below the ≥80% threshold considered acceptable for clinical use, with Claude outperforming ChatGPT-4 by 9 percentage points (p < 0.05) [@Beil2025Comparative]. Similarly, a systematic review analyzing 39 benchmarks across 3,917 screened records found a profound "knowledge-practice gap": leading models achieve 84%–90% accuracy on USMLE-style knowledge examinations, yet practice-based assessments reveal substantially lower performance, with DiagnosisArena at 45.82% (95% CI 42.9%–48.8%), HealthBench at 60%, and safety assessments dropping to 40%–50% despite their life-critical relevance [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice]. The MedHELM evaluation framework, benchmarking nine frontier models across 121 clinical tasks, found that advanced reasoning models DeepSeek R1 and o3-mini achieved win rates of 66%, while Claude 3.5 Sonnet delivered comparable results at 15% lower computational cost [@Bedi2026Holistic].

Agentic clinical decision systems represent a further evolution, but benchmarking reveals that complexity does not always translate to improved accuracy. A study evaluating OpenManus (built on Llama-4) and the proprietary Manus system across the AgentClinic, MedAgentsBench, and Humanity's Last Exam benchmarks found that agent architectures achieved only 60.3% and 28.0% on AgentClinic MedQA and MIMIC tasks, respectively, and 30.3% on MedAgentsBench—modest gains purchased at more than 10-fold greater token consumption and doubled latency, with hallucinations remaining prevalent despite 89.9% being filtered by in-agent safeguards [@Liu2026Benchmarking]. Within gynecologic oncology, a systematic review of 12 retrospective studies found that NLP consultation platforms were less accurate than clinicians overall (60% vs. 86.7%) but that ChatGPT-4 achieved 70% concordance with National Comprehensive Cancer Network guidelines and 75% overall recommendation accuracy in clinical decision support tasks, suggesting that guideline-driven, bounded tasks may represent the most immediate clinical opportunity [@Rosati2026Natural]. The European Federation of Internal Medicine, following a Delphi consensus among 30 international experts, has formally positioned AI as an opportunity for internal medicine, with unanimous consensus endorsing AI-driven clinical decision support as a means to streamline workflows and enhance patient care, while simultaneously urging rigorous real-world validation before widespread adoption [@SaidCriado2025Advancing].

## Electronic Health Records and Clinical Documentation

The administrative burden of clinical documentation represents one of the most tractable targets for LLM implementation, and evidence from multiple prospective studies confirms meaningful productivity gains. A meta-analysis of empirical studies found that automated generative AI-based electronic medical record (EMR) systems reduce documentation time by approximately 40%, while voice recognition and AI scribing technologies reduce patient charting time by 28.8%, collectively reducing the administrative burden underlying physician burnout by more than 30% [@Lim2026How]. Hospital course (HC) summarization has attracted particular scrutiny: a quality improvement study at NYU Langone Health involving 100 general medicine admissions demonstrated that resident physicians edited a significantly smaller proportion of LLM-generated HCs than physician-authored ones (31.5% vs. 44.8%; p < .001), with LLM summaries requiring less semantic change (2.4% vs. 4.9%; p < .001) and rated as more complete by attending hospitalists (mean difference 3.00 on a 10-point scale; p < .001), though with modestly more confabulations (p = .002) [@Small2025Evaluating].

A non-inferiority validation study at a Dutch tertiary academic hospital compared customized LLM-generated summaries with physician-written ones across 50 patient records reviewed by 52 physicians from eight specialties. Completeness and correctness were statistically comparable, overall evaluation scores were similar (3.4 vs. 3.3; p = .373), and 57% of evaluators preferred LLM-generated summaries; crucially, LLMs completed the summarization task in 15.7 seconds versus a physician average of 7 minutes [@Schoonbeek2025Quality]. Ambient AI—systems that passively transcribe and structure clinical encounters—has progressed to pragmatic trial deployment: a framework published in *NEJM AI* documented 65.4% utilization (IQR 50.6–84.0%) of eligible clinical notes completed via ambient AI across 66 providers spanning eight specialties, with an embedded LLM medical coder achieving Pearson's r = 0.97 against professional coders, though a note template change initially caused ICD-10 documentation accuracy to drop from 79% to 35% before recovering after targeted retraining [@Afshar2025A].

Medication documentation from unstructured EHR notes remains a high-stakes extraction challenge. Across three EHR datasets and 12 LLMs, GPT-4o achieved zero-shot F1 scores of 94.0% for medication extraction, 78.1% for discontinuation classification, and 72.7% for the joint task, while open-source models including Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct performed comparably on specific subdatasets, demonstrating viable privacy-preserving alternatives to proprietary systems [@Shao2026Scalable]. For ICD coding—a task with direct financial and quality-reporting implications—a GPT-4o-based system validated against 865,079 assigned codes in the MIMIC-IV database achieved 93.6% accuracy, 95.4% sensitivity, and 85.2% specificity, with a positive predictive value of 84.6% for all codes and 93.9% for principal diagnoses [@Wang2026Validation]. Knowledge-injection strategies substantially modulate coding performance: contextual prompting tailored to medical specialties raised GPT-4o's coding accuracy to 84%–85% for primary and secondary diagnosis tasks, while DeepSeek-V3 with the same strategy achieved 89.5%, 88.6%, and 93.3% across three coding domains [@Jiang2026The]. Structured outcome extraction from EHR text has also been demonstrated: a fine-tuned LLM trained on 2,290 annotated EHR passages achieved 92% accuracy (Cohen κ = 0.84) for binary modified Rankin Scale classification and 77% accuracy (weighted κ = 0.92) for the full seven-level scale, enabling scalable retrospective stroke outcome ascertainment [@Silva2026Assessment].

Beyond structured extraction, LLMs are being applied to clinical prediction. DeLLiriuM, a novel LLM-based delirium prediction model, was developed and validated on 104,303 ICU patients across 195 hospitals from three major databases (eICU, MIMIC-IV, and the University of Florida's Integrated Data Repository), achieving an AUROC of 82.5 (95% CI 81.8–83.1) across 77,543 external validation patients—outperforming all baseline models by transforming structured EHR variables into text-formatted inputs for LLM processing [@Contreras2025DeLLiriuM].

## Clinical Trial Operations

Patient recruitment represents a persistent bottleneck in clinical research, with LLMs increasingly applied to automate and accelerate trial matching. TrialMatchAI, built on fine-tuned open-source LLMs within a retrieval-augmented generation framework, processed heterogeneous clinical records—including structured data and unstructured physician notes—to recommend relevant trials using hybrid lexical and semantic search, chain-of-thought medical reasoning, and criterion-level eligibility assessment. In real-world validation of oncology patients, 92% had at least one relevant trial retrieved within the top 20 recommendations, and expert assessment confirmed over 90% accuracy in criterion-level eligibility classification, with particularly strong performance in biomarker-driven matches [@Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI]. These results establish a compelling benchmark for AI-assisted trial enrollment, though the investigators acknowledge that integration with institutional EHR infrastructure and prospective clinical validation remain necessary next steps.

The accuracy with which LLMs interpret and apply reporting standards also has direct implications for trial operations. A cross-sectional study employing six LLM models to assess 41 RCTs published in JAMA Network Open found that gpt-4-0125-preview achieved the highest Overall Consistency Score against CONSORT-AI criteria (86.5%, 95% CI 82.5%–90.5%), followed by gpt-4-1106-preview (80.3%, 95% CI 76.3%–84.3%), while gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 performed worst (61.9%, 95% CI 57.9%–65.9%); the eligibility criterion item received the lowest average score (48.8%) across all models, pinpointing a specific weakness in eligibility criteria interpretation [@Luo2025Using]. A neuro-symbolic hybrid approach pairing GPT-4 with a rule-based expert system demonstrated that combining LLM flexibility with deterministic verification can reach physician-level performance in structured data extraction: the neuro-symbolic AI achieved F1 scores of 1.00 for both study inclusion and cancer recurrence detection from 5,356 data points across 206 PET/CT reports, compared to GPT-4 alone (F1 0.63 for inclusion, 0.95 for recurrence) and physicians (F1 1.00 and 0.99), while intercepting records containing residual patient identifiers [@Prenosil2025Neurosymbolic].

Data extraction from clinical trial reports is a related high-value task. GPT-4 applied to colorectal cancer imaging reports extracted T, N, and M staging with accuracies of 89%, 90%, and 85%, respectively, and lesion locations with 96% accuracy, achieving an overall TNM stage accuracy of 69%—comparable to human data managers for staging but exceeding them for location accuracy (76% for humans) [@Kim2025Using]. Sentinel lymph node risk prediction from clinical notes using OpenAI's o1 model captured variables in 80% of cases with serial correction, but agreement with physician-calculated nomogram outputs was low (ICC 0.57–0.62); an optimized chain-of-thought GPT-4o prompt improved agreement substantially (ICC 0.95, near-agreement in 90% of cases), illustrating the critical dependence of clinical data extraction tasks on prompt architecture [@Shah2026Integrating].

## Multimodal and Radiology Applications

The integration of LLMs with medical imaging represents one of the most rapidly advancing frontiers in clinical AI. Within radiology, LLMs are being deployed for automated report generation, structured reporting, and patient-centered communication across musculoskeletal, neuroradiology, nuclear medicine, and oncological imaging domains, with expert recommendations emphasizing domain-specific training and hallucination mitigation as prerequisites for safe clinical integration [@Park2025Clinical]. In histopathology and scientific figure interpretation, ChatGPT-4 significantly outperformed Gemini Advanced and Copilot across five evaluation parameters—relevance, clarity, depth, focus, and coherence—for both histopathological slides and scientific images (p < 0.001 for all comparisons), suggesting utility in pathology education and potentially in diagnostic support [@Gumilar2026Assessing].

Ophthalmology has seen the development of domain-specific LLMs: LEME (Language Enhanced Model for Eye), pre-trained on Llama2-70B and fine-tuned on approximately 127,000 ophthalmology-specific instances, outperformed GPT-4 and seven other models in internal validation tasks, achieving fill-in-the-blank Rouge-L scores of 0.82 ± 0.04 (p < 0.0001 vs. all comparators) and ranked highest in EHR summarization and clinical QA with correctness scores of 4.24–4.83 out of 5, demonstrating the performance gains achievable through domain-specific fine-tuning [@Gilson2024Language]. Retrieval-augmented generation applied to nuclear medicine—evaluated using a Japanese institutional nuclear medicine manual—further established the viability of grounding LLM outputs in institutional knowledge sources for specialized imaging consultation [@Fukui2025Evaluation].

A particularly provocative development concerns the authenticity of medical images themselves. A diagnostic accuracy study involving 17 radiologists from six countries evaluated the ability to distinguish 77 ChatGPT-generated (GPT-4o) synthetic radiographs from 77 authentic clinical images across multiple anatomic regions, with the same task assigned to GPT-4o, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Llama 4 Maverick [@Tordjman2026The]. The rise of deepfake medical imaging presents a dual challenge: while LLMs automate legitimate imaging workflows, their capacity to generate photorealistic synthetic radiographs introduces risks for misuse, fraud, and research data integrity that must be addressed through watermarking and regulatory frameworks before multimodal deployment reaches its full clinical potential. The international REFINE consensus guideline, developed by 57 contributors from 17 countries through a modified Delphi process, provides a 44-item, six-section reporting framework specifically designed to ensure transparent and reproducible evaluation of foundation and large language models in medical imaging research, offering a basis for standardized evidence synthesis across this rapidly evolving landscape [@Mese2026Reporting].

Taken together, LLMs are already delivering measurable value across clinical decision support, documentation, trial operations, and imaging, but performance is highly task-specific, model-dependent, and sensitive to prompt design and domain adaptation. The gap between benchmark performance and real-world clinical reliability remains the central challenge for safe translation.
